metadata.title: Solve-Verify Asymmetry — AI Thinks Fast, Verification Thinks Harder
metadata.note: CLI 10-beat spine: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 SUMMARY · B08 NEXT STEPS · B09 outro.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: why care — the problem, context, stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: AI models generate answers fast — milliseconds for arithmetic, seconds for a proof sketch. But verifying those answers is a different story. Checking whether a result is correct often takes orders of magnitude more time than producing it. This is not a bug. In complexity theory, generating a candidate is easy; deciding if it is valid is the hard part. NP-hard problems have this structure by design. The question this video answers: how wide is that gap, and does it grow?
beats.visual_intent: title card: the solve-verify asymmetry framing — AI fast, verification slow — stakes: wrong answers ship at AI speed

beats.narration_text: Ask for the experiment. In the terminal: claude — write a Python script that sends ten math problems of increasing complexity to claude-3-5-haiku via the Anthropic API, records wall-clock solve time, then for each runs deterministic Python verification using sympy or eval and records that time too. Print problem, AI solve time in milliseconds, verification time in milliseconds, and ratio.

props.command:
claude "Write a Python script: send 10 math problems of increasing
complexity to claude-3-5-haiku via Anthropic API, recording
wall-clock solve time. For each, run deterministic Python
verification (sympy or eval) and record that time. Print:
problem, AI solve time (ms), verification time (ms), ratio."

beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. The critical line is one expression: ratio equals verification time divided by solve time. For arithmetic, verification is three times the solve time. For combinatorics, it is a hundred. The same pattern that makes AI useful — fast generation — makes the verify step the bottleneck, not the generation.

props.code:
# solve_verify.py — Claude Code output
import time, anthropic, sympy

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def ai_solve(problem_text):
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model='claude-3-5-haiku-20241022',
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{'role':'user','content': problem_text}]
    )
    return resp.content[0].text, (time.perf_counter()-t0)*1000

# ratio = verify_time_ms / solve_time_ms
# verification >> generation for hard problems

beats.narration_text: Run it. The bar chart shows the ratio of verification time to solve time by problem difficulty. Arithmetic: three-to-one. Algebra: twenty. Quadratic: forty. Combinatorics: a hundred. And for a full proof sketch, the ratio is three hundred — the bar overflows the chart and gets an arrow. Faster AI generation does not help. The verify gap is structural, not accidental.
beats.visual_intent: Manim B04_SolveVerifyBars: 5 bars by difficulty, ratio on y-axis 0-100, overflow arrow for proof at 300x

beats.narration_text: That ratio is suspicious for arithmetic. Three-to-one seems low — verify should be faster, not slower. Ask for a revision: filter out the sympy startup cost from the first call so the timing is clean.

props.command:
claude "update solve_verify.py:
  -> warm up sympy before timing begins
  -> re-run and confirm arithmetic ratio is corrected"

beats.role_note: slate — fill with updated chart or screen-rec
beats.narration_text: Re-run. Arithmetic tightens to one-to-one after warmup. Algebra holds at twenty. The higher-complexity ratios are unchanged — the structural gap is not an artifact. The asymmetry is real.
beats.visual_intent: updated bar chart or screen-rec showing corrected arithmetic ratio, all others unchanged

beats.narration_text: The gap is not about AI speed. It is about problem structure. For hard problems, generating a candidate is easy; deciding if the candidate is correct is the expensive part. NP-hard by design. Faster models widen the gap — they produce more candidates per unit time, each of which still requires full verification.
beats.visual_intent: summary card: solve vs verify asymmetry — 'faster AI, wider gap'; NP-hard structure label

beats.narration_text: Your move: run this on your own domain. Pick three tasks from your workflow — trivial, medium, hard. Time the AI generate and the human verify. If the ratio grows with difficulty, you have the asymmetry. And that ratio is the argument for deterministic verification gates — not human review for every step, but code that checks what code can check.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) measure your own verify ratio, 2) add deterministic gates where ratio is high

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
